verses.text: Then sang Deborah and Barak son of Abinoam on that day, saying: Praise ye the LORD for the avenging of Israel, when the people willingly offered themselves. Hear, O ye kings; give ear, O ye princes; I, even I, will sing unto the LORD; I will sing praise to the LORD God of Israel. Guidance: One of the Bible's oldest poems and certainly its earliest battle-song. The two leaders sing together — a prophet and a general, sharing the same praise. The opening note is not strategy or strength but willingness: 'when the people willingly offered themselves.' Volunteers, not conscripts, are God's preferred army. The next note is the singer's own resolve: 'I, even I' — Deborah will lead the worship as she led the battle.

LORD, when thou wentest out of Seir, when thou marchedst out of the field of Edom, the earth trembled, and the heavens dropped, the clouds also dropped water. The mountains melted from before the LORD, even that Sinai from before the LORD God of Israel. Guidance: Deborah's song does not start with Sisera; it starts with Sinai. The God who shook the earth at the giving of the law is the same God who shook the Kishon valley today. The poem ties the immediate victory to the foundational covenant. The 'clouds dropped water' (v4) and 'Kishon swept them away' (v21) bracket the song with rain — the storm that flooded the chariots is heaven's old weapon, used again.

In the days of Shamgar son of Anath, in the days of Jael, the highways were unoccupied, and the travellers walked through byways. The inhabitants of the villages ceased, they ceased in Israel, until that I Deborah arose, that I arose a mother in Israel. They chose new gods; then was war in the gates: was there a shield or spear seen among forty thousand in Israel? Guidance: A snapshot of life under Jabin. Roads abandoned, smugglers' trails used instead, villages emptying out because no one was safe. Idolatry produced the conditions: 'They chose new gods; then was war.' And then Deborah's title: not 'judge,' not 'prophetess,' but 'mother in Israel.' A nation in crisis needed not just a commander but a mother — protective, present, fierce on behalf of her children. Some callings are written in mother-tongues.

My heart is toward the governors of Israel, that offered themselves willingly among the people. Bless ye the LORD. Speak, ye that ride on white asses, ye that sit in judgment, and walk by the way. They that are delivered from the noise of archers in the places of drawing water, there shall they rehearse the righteous acts of the LORD, even the righteous acts toward the inhabitants of his villages in Israel: then shall the people of the LORD go down to the gates. Guidance: The poem widens to thank the willing leaders, then invites everyone — riders of white donkeys (the wealthy), judges, ordinary walkers, and women at the wells — to retell what God did. The 'places of drawing water' (where the village women gathered) become the village storytelling circles. The gospel is recited where the women fetch the water. Faith spreads at well-mouths and supper tables more than at the city gates.

Awake, awake, Deborah: awake, awake, utter a song: arise, Barak, and lead thy captivity captive, thou son of Abinoam. Then he made him that remaineth have dominion over the nobles among the people: the LORD made me have dominion over the mighty. Guidance: A self-summons. Deborah calls herself to wake up — even prophets must rouse themselves to sing. Barak gets a stirring command quoted later by Paul (Eph 4:8): 'lead thy captivity captive.' The poem credits both: Barak leads, but the LORD makes the people dominate the mighty. Two agents, one outcome. Worship songs do not flatten the human chain of cause; they keep adding 'the LORD' to every link.

Out of Ephraim was there a root of them against Amalek; after thee, Benjamin, among thy people; out of Machir came down governors, and out of Zebulun they that handle the pen of the writer. The princes of Issachar were with Deborah; even Issachar, and also Barak: he was sent on foot into the valley. For the divisions of Reuben there were great thoughts of heart. Guidance: A roll-call of who showed up. Six tribes named for stepping in: Ephraim, Benjamin, Machir (western Manasseh), Zebulun (with even the scribes — 'they that handle the pen of the writer'), Issachar, and Naphtali implicitly (where Barak was from). 'Sent on foot into the valley' — Barak charged downhill against chariots, a brave act of trust. Names of the faithful get sung into the public record; God's worship has roll-calls.

Why abodest thou among the sheepfolds, to hear the bleatings of the flocks? For the divisions of Reuben there were great searchings of heart. Gilead abode beyond Jordan: and why did Dan remain in ships? Asher continued on the sea shore, and abode in his breaches. Zebulun and Naphtali were a people that jeoparded their lives unto the death in the high places of the field. Guidance: Four tribes named for staying home: Reuben (still mulling at the sheepfolds, 'great searchings of heart' — Hebrew sarcasm), Gilead (eastern Manasseh and Gad, comfortably across the river), Dan (busy with shipping), Asher (lounging on the coast). Then in deliberate contrast: Zebulun and Naphtali 'jeoparded their lives unto the death.' The song uses public praise as social pressure. To be left out of the LORD's roll of the brave is itself the rebuke. Every generation has its sheepfold-tribe.

The kings came and fought, then fought the kings of Canaan in Taanach by the waters of Megiddo; they took no gain of money. They fought from heaven; the stars in their courses fought against Sisera. The river of Kishon swept them away, that ancient river, the river Kishon. O my soul, thou hast trodden down strength. Guidance: The poem's most majestic lines. The Canaanite kings expected to plunder; they got nothing. Why? 'They fought from heaven; the stars in their courses fought against Sisera.' This is what the prose of chapter 4 only hinted at: the LORD turned creation itself into the army. The Kishon — usually a sluggish stream — flash-flooded, the chariots became coffins. Deborah's soul finally vents what she had to keep silent through twenty years of oppression: 'O my soul, thou hast trodden down strength.'

Then were the horsehoofs broken by the means of the pransings, the pransings of their mighty ones. Curse ye Meroz, said the angel of the LORD, curse ye bitterly the inhabitants thereof; because they came not to the help of the LORD, to the help of the LORD against the mighty. Guidance: The horses, prancing wildly in the mud, broke their own hooves. Even animal panic served God's design. Then a sharp curse: Meroz — a town we never hear of again, perhaps a small village on the Kishon's banks that could have helped the fleeing remnants of Israel but stayed neutral. The angel of the LORD himself signs the curse. Neutrality during the LORD's war is itself a verdict. Some towns disappear from history precisely because they did not show up in it.

Blessed above women shall Jael the wife of Heber the Kenite be, blessed shall she be above women in the tent. He asked water, and she gave him milk; she brought forth butter in a lordly dish. She put her hand to the nail, and her right hand to the workmen's hammer; with the hammer she smote Sisera, she smote off his head, when she had pierced and stricken through his temples. At her feet he bowed, he fell, he lay down: at her feet he bowed, he fell: where he bowed, there he fell down dead. Guidance: 'Blessed above women' — the same construction Gabriel will use of Mary (Luke 1:42). Two foreign women, two superlatives, both serving God's deliverance plan. The poem's most repeated word is 'fell.' He bowed, he fell, he lay down — five verbs, three lines, slowed to dirge tempo. The general who once made Israel bow now bows. The chapter's worst tyrant gets the chapter's most ridiculous obituary.

The mother of Sisera looked out at a window, and cried through the lattice, Why is his chariot so long in coming? why tarry the wheels of his chariots? Her wise ladies answered her, yea, she returned answer to herself, Have they not sped? have they not divided the prey; to every man a damsel or two; to every man of divers colours, a prey of divers colours of needlework, of divers colours of needlework on both sides, meet for the necks of them that take the spoil? Guidance: The chapter's most haunting scene. Sisera's mother peers through the latticed window, voicing the question every mother of a soldier asks: 'why is he late?' Then the song imagines what her ladies-in-waiting say to calm her — a chilling answer. They speculate his delay is plunder: damsels (literally 'a womb or two per man' — the Hebrew is even cruder), embroidered scarves to wear home. The dead general's mother imagines her son raping the very wives and mothers his army was destroying. The song wants us to feel the moral cost of war — even on the side of the conquered. Jael's hammer ended that day's atrocities. Sisera's mother will never know why he is late.

So let all thine enemies perish, O LORD: but let them that love him be as the sun when he goeth forth in his might. And the land had rest forty years. Guidance: The song ends with two pictures stacked. May the LORD's enemies fade like Sisera; may those who love Him be like the rising sun. The same sky that brought hailstones for one army brings sunlight for another. And the closing prose: 'the land had rest forty years.' Forty years — a generation. The poem ends; the years count out the answer to it. The LORD's enemies have a sunset; His friends have a sunrise. Choose which side of the horizon to stand on.
